name: Use GitHub Copilot Features
tagline: Completions, chat, and the modes — plus agents, CLI, MCP, Spaces, Spark, and code review. Pick the right surface for the job.
overview: The heaviest domain on GH-300 (25–30%). It tests whether you can use Copilot's full feature set well: inline completions and chat, the Ask/Edit/Agent interaction modes and Plan Mode, the coding agent and Copilot CLI, grounding with MCP and Spaces, building with Spark, and Copilot code review — and whether you can choose the right surface for a given task and operate agents responsibly. The January 2026 refresh put agents, Plan Mode, MCP, Spaces, Spark, and code review squarely in scope.
objectives: Use completions and chat, and choose between the Ask, Edit, and Agent interaction modes (plus Plan Mode) for a task.
Operate the coding agent and Copilot CLI, and know that agent output lands as a reviewable pull request, not a direct push.
Ground Copilot in real context with MCP servers and Copilot Spaces, and know when each fits.
Apply code review, PR summaries, and Spark, framing AI output as assistive with humans accountable.
Manage features across an organisation with policies, and reason about availability by plan and IDE.

lessons.title: The surfaces: completions, chat, and the three modes
lessons.objective: Match the task to the right Copilot surface — completions, chat, or the Ask/Agent/Plan modes.
lessons.body:
## Three ways Copilot shows up

- **Code completions** — inline 'ghost text' as you type. Accept with Tab. Best for staying in flow: finishing a line, a boilerplate block, an obvious next step.
- **Copilot Chat** — a conversation about your code. Ask questions, get explanations, request changes. Add context with `#` references (files, symbols) and `@` participants like `@workspace`; use slash commands such as `/explain`, `/fix`, and `/tests`.
- **The interaction modes** inside chat set how much Copilot *does*, not just says.

## Ask, Edit, Agent — and Plan

- **Ask mode** — Copilot answers and explains. It proposes, but you apply changes. Reach for it to understand code, weigh options, or learn.
- **Edit mode** — you nominate a set of files and Copilot edits across just those, which you review inline before keeping. More active than Ask, more contained than Agent — good for a focused, multi-file change you want on a tight leash.
- **Agent mode** — Copilot acts autonomously: it decides which files to touch, can run terminal commands and tests, and iterates until the goal is met. You review the result. Reach for it for open-ended, multi-step work.
- **Plan mode** — Copilot first drafts a step-by-step plan you approve, *then* executes it. Reach for it when a change is big or ambiguous and you want to catch a wrong approach before any code is written.

> **Exam note:** the GH-300 blueprint (January 2026) lists **Ask, Edit, and Agent** modes plus **Plan Mode** — know what each does. In the product, GitHub has been consolidating Edit into Agent mode through 2026, so day to day you lean on Ask → Plan → Agent; for the exam, still be ready for Edit mode.

## Why the exam cares

The biggest slice of the blueprint. Scenario questions hand you a task and ask which surface fits. The instinct to grade for: **use the lightest surface that does the job** — completions and Ask for small or read-only work, Plan then Agent for larger changes — and always review what an agent produces.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You want Copilot to explain what an unfamiliar function does, without changing any code. Which surface fits best?

options.text: Chat in Ask mode (e.g. /explain with the function referenced)
Agent mode, so it can refactor the function
Inline completions
The coding agent via a GitHub issue

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Understanding code with no edits is exactly what **Ask mode** is for — `/explain` with a `#`-reference to the function. Agent mode would start changing code; completions won't explain; the coding agent is for background tasks that end in a PR.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A change touches three files and needs a test run to confirm it works. Which mode is the natural fit?

options.text: Agent mode — it edits across files and can run the tests, iterating until done
Ask mode — it will apply the multi-file change itself
Completions — accept enough ghost text and the change appears
None — Copilot cannot edit more than one file

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Multi-file work with a verification step is **Agent mode**: it makes the edits, runs the tests, and iterates. Ask mode proposes but doesn't apply; completions are line-level; Copilot can absolutely edit many files.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What does Plan mode add over jumping straight into Agent mode?

options.text: A reviewable step-by-step plan you approve before any code is written, so a wrong approach is caught early
It runs the change without a language model, so it's deterministic
It merges the resulting pull request automatically
It disables tests to run faster

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Plan mode surfaces Copilot's intended approach as a plan you approve first — cheap insurance against it heading the wrong way on a big or ambiguous task. It doesn't remove the model, auto-merge, or skip tests.

lessons.title: Agents: coding agent, Plan mode, and the Copilot CLI
lessons.objective: Operate Copilot's agents and internalise that their output is always reviewed, never auto-merged.
lessons.body:
## From assistant to agent

Agent mode in the IDE is Copilot acting on a goal. Two more agent surfaces extend that:

- **The coding agent** (cloud/background agent). Assign a GitHub issue to Copilot, or ask it to take a task; it works asynchronously in its own environment and opens a **pull request** with its changes. You review, comment, and it can revise the PR — the same review gate any human contributor goes through. It never pushes straight to a protected branch.
- **Copilot CLI**. An agent in your terminal. It can run shell and `git` commands, explore a repo, and carry out multi-step tasks from the command line — with the same review-before-you-trust discipline for anything destructive.

## Plan, then act

For a substantial task, have the agent produce a **plan** first (Plan mode), approve or edit it, then let it execute. This keeps long agent runs on track and gives you an artifact to reason about.

## The cardinal operating rule

Agents **propose changes you review** — they don't merge for you. The whole model is: Copilot does the work in a branch/PR/session, and a human stays accountable for what lands. Treat 'the agent opened a PR' as the *start* of review, not the end.

## When to reach for the coding agent

Well-scoped, independently verifiable chores are the sweet spot: a dependency bump with tests, a small well-specified feature, boilerplate across files. Hand it a clear issue and acceptance criteria; get back a PR to review. Vague, high-stakes, or architectural work still wants a human at the wheel.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You assign a GitHub issue to the Copilot coding agent. What do you get back?

options.text: A pull request with its changes, for you to review
A direct commit pushed to main
A merged release, deployed automatically
Only a chat message describing what it would do

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The coding agent works in the background and opens a **pull request** — the human review gate is the point. It doesn't push to main or deploy on its own.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which task is the best fit to hand to the coding agent rather than doing it interactively?

options.text: A well-specified dependency upgrade with existing tests to prove it
Deciding the system's core architecture
A production incident needing a judgment call right now
A vague 'make the app better' request

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Background agents shine on scoped, verifiable chores — a tested dependency bump. Architecture, live incidents, and vague asks need human judgment and shouldn't be delegated blind.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What is the Copilot CLI for?

options.text: Running Copilot as an agent in the terminal for shell, git, and multi-step tasks
Installing IDE extensions from the command line
Training a custom model on your repository
Replacing the GitHub REST API

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Copilot CLI brings the agent to the terminal — it can run shell and git commands and carry out multi-step work from the command line. It isn't an extension installer, a training tool, or an API replacement.

lessons.title: Grounding Copilot: MCP servers and Copilot Spaces
lessons.objective: Give Copilot real context with MCP and Spaces, and choose the right one.
lessons.body:
## Context is the lever

Generic answers come from missing context. Two features close the gap beyond `#`-references and `@workspace`:

- **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** — an open standard for connecting Copilot to *external* tools and data: issue trackers, databases, observability, your own internal services. You configure an MCP server and Copilot (in chat, agent mode, or the CLI) can pull live context and take actions through it. GitHub ships an MCP server for GitHub data; you can add others. Reach for MCP when Copilot needs a **live system** — real tickets, real query results, real actions.
- **Copilot Spaces** — a curated bundle of context (repositories, specific files, docs, free-text notes) that grounds chat for a task or team. Ask questions inside a Space and answers are specific to *your* system and conventions, and shareable with teammates. Reach for a Space when you want to **curate knowledge** — onboarding, a subsystem's rules, a project's context.

## MCP vs Spaces in one line

**MCP connects a live tool or data source; a Space curates a knowledge bundle.** They compose — a Space can set the scene while an MCP server fetches live data.

## Why the exam cares

The refresh made both first-class. Expect questions that hand you a grounding problem — 'answers ignore our internal API', 'the team keeps re-explaining the same architecture' — and ask which feature fixes it.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You want Copilot to read live tickets from your issue tracker and your internal deployment tool while it works. Which feature is designed for that?

options.text: MCP servers — connect external tools/data via the open protocol
A Copilot Space of screenshots
Raising the completion length
Switching the chat model

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Connecting Copilot to live external systems is **MCP** — configure a server for the tool/data and Copilot can pull context and act through it. A Space curates static context; model choice and length don't add data sources.

knowledgeCheck.stem: New joiners keep asking the same questions about a service's conventions. You want Copilot answers grounded in that service's docs and code for the whole team. What fits?

options.text: A Copilot Space bundling the service's repo, docs, and notes, shared with the team
An MCP server that trains a bespoke model nightly
Telling everyone to paste the docs into each chat
Turning off content exclusions

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Curating a reusable, shareable context bundle is a **Copilot Space**. MCP connects live tools (and doesn't train models); pasting docs every time doesn't scale; content exclusions are unrelated.

lessons.title: Build and review at scale: Spark, code review, PR summaries, and org management
lessons.objective: Use Spark, code review, and PR summaries as assistive tools, and manage features across an org.
lessons.body:
## Spark: from prose to a running app

**GitHub Spark** turns a natural-language description into a full-stack app it can build and deploy, integrated with GitHub. It's built for prototypes and quick internal tools — get to something running fast, then iterate.

## Review and summaries: assistive, not accountable

- **Copilot code review** — request an AI review on a pull request and it comments on issues it spots; some setups expose an effort level for depth. It **augments** human review, catching easy misses and speeding the loop — it does not replace the human reviewer who is accountable for the merge.
- **PR summaries** — Copilot drafts the pull-request description from the diff, so reviewers get context faster. You still edit and own it.

## Managing features across an organisation

Admins control Copilot with **policies**: enable or disable features (e.g. agents, CLI, code review) for the org or teams, manage seats, and set guardrails. Feature **availability varies by plan** (Free, Pro, Business, Enterprise) and by **IDE** — the feature matrix tells you what's where. A rollout decision is as much 'which policies and plan' as 'which feature'.

## Why the exam cares

The blueprint folds organisation-wide management and the feature matrix into this domain. Expect 'as an admin, how do you enforce X' and 'is feature Y available on plan Z / in IDE W' framing — and the recurring theme that AI review/summaries are assistive with humans accountable.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Your team turns on Copilot code review for every PR. What's the correct way to frame it?

options.text: An assistive first pass that augments human review; a person is still accountable for the merge
A replacement for human reviewers, so approvals can be automated
A guarantee the code is bug-free once it comments
A tool that merges the PR when it finds no issues

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Copilot code review speeds the loop and catches easy misses, but it's **assistive** — humans remain accountable for what merges. It's not a replacement, a correctness guarantee, or an auto-merger.

knowledgeCheck.stem: An admin needs to disable the Copilot CLI for one team while keeping chat enabled. How is that done?

options.text: Through organisation/enterprise Copilot policies that toggle features by scope
By uninstalling the CLI from each developer's machine manually
It can't be done — features are all-or-nothing
By downgrading everyone to the Free plan

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Feature enablement is governed by **policies** at the org/enterprise level, scoped to teams. It isn't manual per-machine, all-or-nothing, or tied to forcing a plan downgrade.

questions.stem: Which surface is best for quickly accepting a one-line, obvious next statement while typing?

options.text: Inline code completions
The coding agent
Plan mode
A Copilot Space

questions.explanation: Line-level, in-flow suggestions are **completions** (accept with Tab). Agents, Plan mode, and Spaces are heavier surfaces for larger or context-grounding work.

questions.stem: A developer asks Copilot to implement a feature spanning several files and run the tests. Which mode is designed for this?

options.text: Agent mode
Ask mode
Completions only
PR summaries

questions.explanation: Multi-file implementation with test execution is **Agent mode**. Ask mode proposes without applying, completions are line-level, and PR summaries describe an existing diff.

questions.stem: Why use Plan mode before a large refactor?

options.text: To review and approve Copilot's intended approach before any code changes
To skip code review afterwards
To force the cheapest model
To disable content exclusions for the repo

questions.explanation: Plan mode makes the approach explicit and approvable up front — you catch a wrong direction before edits. It doesn't remove review, pick a model, or touch exclusions.

questions.stem: The Copilot coding agent finishes a task. Where do its changes appear?

options.text: In a pull request for human review
Committed directly to the default branch
Deployed straight to production
Only in the agent's private memory

questions.explanation: The coding agent opens a **pull request** — review is built into the workflow. It does not push to main or deploy autonomously.

questions.stem: Which is the strongest candidate to delegate to the background coding agent?

options.text: Bumping a library version and fixing the call sites, with tests to verify
Choosing between two competing architectures
Responding to a live outage
An open-ended 'improve performance somewhere' task

questions.explanation: Scoped, testable chores are ideal for the coding agent. Architecture choices, live incidents, and vague asks need human judgment.

questions.stem: You need Copilot to query your internal database and read live tickets while assisting. What's the right mechanism?

options.text: Configure MCP servers for those systems
Paste rows and tickets into the prompt each time
Create a Space of static screenshots
Switch to inline completions

questions.explanation: Live external tools/data are connected via **MCP**. Manual pasting doesn't scale, static screenshots aren't live, and completions add no data source.

questions.stem: A team wants Copilot chat answers grounded in one service's repo, docs, and conventions, reusable by everyone. Which feature?

options.text: A shared Copilot Space
An MCP server that fine-tunes a model
A longer system prompt pasted per chat
Turning off duplication detection

questions.explanation: Curating shareable, task-specific context is a **Copilot Space**. MCP connects live tools (and doesn't fine-tune); per-chat pasting doesn't scale; duplication detection is unrelated.

options.text: Generates and can deploy a full-stack app from a natural-language description
Reviews pull requests for security issues
Stores organisation-wide Copilot policies
Excludes files from Copilot

questions.explanation: **Spark** builds (and can deploy) full-stack apps from prose — great for prototypes and internal tools. The other options describe code review, admin policies, and content exclusion.

questions.stem: How should Copilot code review sit in a team's process?

options.text: As an assistive pass alongside human review, which remains accountable for merges
As the sole approver, replacing human reviewers
As a guarantee of correctness
As an auto-merge bot

questions.explanation: AI review augments humans; it doesn't replace the accountable reviewer, guarantee correctness, or merge on its own.

questions.stem: What do Copilot PR summaries produce?

options.text: A draft pull-request description generated from the diff
A merge decision
A new MCP server
A deployment pipeline

questions.explanation: PR summaries **draft the description** from the changes so reviewers get context faster. You still edit and own it; it doesn't merge, add servers, or deploy.

questions.stem: An enterprise admin wants agents enabled for engineering but off for a contractor team. What's the mechanism?

options.text: Organisation/enterprise Copilot policies scoped by team
Asking each contractor not to use agents
Deleting the agent feature from GitHub
Moving contractors to a personal plan

questions.explanation: Feature enablement by scope is governed by **policies**. Honour systems, deletion, and plan-juggling aren't the control surface.

questions.stem: A developer says 'Copilot agent mode isn't available in my setup'. What's the first thing to check?

options.text: Whether their plan and IDE support the feature, and whether org policy enables it
Whether they have enough disk space
Whether the repository is public
Whether they cleared their browser cache

questions.explanation: Availability depends on **plan, IDE, and org policy** — the feature matrix plus admin settings. Disk, repo visibility, and cache aren't the gating factors.

questions.stem: Which pairing of task → surface is the best match?

options.text: Understand a bug in unfamiliar code → Chat/Ask with @workspace
Accept a boilerplate line → the coding agent
Explain one function → GitHub Spark
Toggle a team's features → inline completions

questions.explanation: Ask mode with workspace context is right for understanding unfamiliar code. The others are mismatched: boilerplate is completions, explaining is Ask (not Spark), and feature toggles are admin policies.

questions.stem: Which are sound reasons to run a task through Plan mode before Agent mode? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The change is large and the approach is uncertain
You want to review and adjust the steps before edits happen
You want an artifact that documents the intended approach
You want to bypass pull-request review afterwards

questions.explanation: Plan mode is for scoping big/ambiguous changes, reviewing steps first, and producing a plan artifact. It never removes downstream code review.

questions.stem: MCP and Copilot Spaces — which statement is correct?

options.text: MCP connects Copilot to live external tools/data; a Space curates a reusable context bundle
They are two names for the same feature
Spaces connect live databases; MCP stores static notes
Both require training a custom model

questions.explanation: **MCP = live connections; Spaces = curated context.** They're distinct and composable, and neither trains a model.

questions.stem: Which slash command asks Copilot Chat to propose tests for the selected code?

options.text: /tests
/deploy
/merge
/exclude

questions.explanation: `/tests` requests tests for the current selection/context. The others aren't Copilot Chat test commands.

questions.stem: You want Copilot in the terminal to run a sequence of git operations for you. Which surface?

options.text: Copilot CLI
A Copilot Space
PR summaries
Inline completions

questions.explanation: Terminal-native, multi-step agentic work is **Copilot CLI**. Spaces curate context, PR summaries describe diffs, and completions are in-editor.

questions.stem: A reviewer says 'the coding agent's PR passed Copilot code review, so we can skip human review'. What's the flaw?

options.text: Both are assistive; a human must still be accountable for what merges
Copilot code review only runs on private repos
The coding agent cannot open PRs
Nothing — AI review replaces human review

questions.explanation: Stacking two assistive tools doesn't remove accountability. The coding agent proposes; AI review augments — a human still owns the merge decision.

questions.stem: On the GH-300 blueprint, how does Edit mode differ from Agent mode?

options.text: Edit mode changes a set of files you nominate and review inline; Agent mode works more autonomously — choosing files and running commands until the goal is met
Edit mode only answers questions and never changes code
Agent mode is read-only; Edit mode runs the terminal
They are two names for the same feature

questions.explanation: Edit mode applies changes across a scoped set of files you choose and review inline; Agent mode is more autonomous — it decides which files to touch and can run commands/tests, iterating to the goal. Ask mode is the read-only, explain-only surface. In the product GitHub has been folding Edit into Agent, but the January-2026 exam blueprint still lists Ask, Edit, Agent, and Plan — so know all four.

scenarios.title: Ship a feature with the right surfaces
scenarios.tagline: Match each step of real work to the right Copilot surface.
scenarios.context: You're adding a rate-limiter to an unfamiliar service. You'll need to understand the code, make a cross-file change, pull config from an internal system, and hand off a follow-up chore. Pick the surface for each.

steps.prompt: First you need to understand how requests currently flow, without touching code. What do you use?

choices.text: Chat in Ask mode with @workspace to explain the request path
choices.rationale: Read-only understanding of unfamiliar code is exactly Ask mode with workspace context.

choices.text: Agent mode, and let it refactor as it explains
choices.rationale: You explicitly don't want edits yet; Agent mode will start changing code.

choices.text: Inline completions while you scroll
choices.rationale: Completions help while typing but won't give you a structured explanation of the flow.

steps.prompt: Now implement the limiter across the middleware, config, and tests. How?

choices.text: Plan mode to agree the approach, then Agent mode to implement and run tests
choices.rationale: A multi-file change with tests benefits from Plan-then-Agent: agree the approach, then let the agent execute and verify.

choices.text: Ask mode and copy each suggested snippet in by hand
choices.rationale: Works, but you're doing the multi-file application manually — Agent mode is what that's for.

choices.text: Accept completions until the feature emerges
choices.rationale: Line-level completions can't reliably carry a coordinated cross-file change with verification.

steps.prompt: The limits live in an internal config service. You want Copilot to read the real values. What do you set up?

choices.text: An MCP server for the config service so Copilot can pull live values
choices.rationale: Live external data is MCP's job — connect the config service and Copilot reads real values.

choices.text: Paste the current config into the chat once
choices.rationale: Fine for a one-off, but it goes stale and doesn't scale to the team or to actions.

choices.text: A Space of last quarter's config screenshots
choices.rationale: Static, stale screenshots aren't the live source of truth this step needs.

steps.prompt: There's a tidy follow-up: add the same limiter to two sibling services, well specified, with tests. How do you hand it off?

choices.text: Open issues and assign them to the coding agent; review the PRs it opens
choices.rationale: Scoped, testable, independent chores are the coding agent's sweet spot — you review the resulting PRs.

choices.text: Do both interactively in Agent mode yourself right now
choices.rationale: Perfectly fine, just not the most leveraged use of your time for well-specified, parallelisable work.

choices.text: Let the coding agent push straight to main to save a review cycle
choices.rationale: The agent opens PRs for review by design; bypassing review defeats the safety model.

scenarios.title: Roll Copilot out across an engineering org
scenarios.tagline: Policies, grounding, and review as an admin decision.
scenarios.context: You're the platform lead enabling Copilot for 300 engineers plus a contractor team. You must control which features are on, ground answers in internal systems, and slot AI into the PR process — responsibly.

steps.prompt: Engineering gets agents and CLI; the contractor team should have chat only. How do you enforce it?

choices.text: Set organisation/enterprise Copilot policies, scoped per team
choices.rationale: Policies are the control surface for enabling features by team scope.

choices.text: Ask the contractors not to use agents
choices.rationale: An honour system isn't enforcement; use policy.

choices.text: Give contractors a separate, lower plan and hope features differ
choices.rationale: Plan differences do gate some features, but policy is the precise, intentional control here.

steps.prompt: Answers ignore your internal frameworks and conventions. How do you ground them for everyone?

choices.text: Publish shared Copilot Spaces for key systems, and add MCP servers for live tools
choices.rationale: Spaces curate the conventions; MCP connects the live systems — together they ground answers org-wide.

choices.text: Tell each engineer to paste the conventions into every chat
choices.rationale: Doesn't scale and isn't consistent — exactly what Spaces solve.

choices.text: Only raise the model tier
choices.rationale: A stronger model still lacks your private context; grounding is the real fix.

steps.prompt: You want AI to help with reviews without weakening accountability. What's the policy?

choices.text: Enable Copilot code review as an assistive first pass; keep a human approver required to merge
choices.rationale: AI augments the review and a human stays accountable — the responsible pattern.

choices.text: Let Copilot code review auto-approve PRs it likes
choices.rationale: Auto-approval removes the accountable human — not acceptable.

choices.text: Use PR summaries only, no AI review
choices.rationale: Summaries help context but leave the review-quality gain on the table; both can coexist.

labs.title: Drive a change through Ask → Plan → Agent
labs.goal: Feel the difference between the modes on one real change.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

Take a small feature request in a repo you know and move it through all three modes.

1. **Ask** — in chat, use `/explain` and `@workspace` to understand where the change belongs. Make no edits.
2. **Plan** — switch to Plan mode and have Copilot draft the steps. Read them critically; edit anything wrong before approving.
3. **Agent** — execute the approved plan in Agent mode. Let it edit the files and run the tests; watch it iterate.
4. **Review** — read the diff yourself as if it were a teammate's PR. Accept, refine, or reject.

## Why it matters

The exam rewards choosing the lightest surface that works and always reviewing agent output. Doing all three back-to-back makes the trade-offs concrete.
checklist: Ask mode produced understanding with zero edits
You edited the plan before approving it
Agent mode made the change and ran tests
You reviewed the diff before accepting

labs.title: Ground Copilot with an MCP server and a Space
labs.goal: See live context (MCP) and curated context (Spaces) side by side.
labs.steps:
## Steps

1. Configure the **GitHub MCP server** in your IDE so Copilot can read issues/PRs for a repo. Ask a question that requires a live issue — confirm it pulls the real data.
2. Create a **Copilot Space** bundling that repo plus a short conventions doc and notes. Ask a question about the repo's conventions and confirm the answer reflects them.
3. Contrast: note which question needed *live* data (MCP) and which needed *curated* context (Space).

## Checkpoint

If you tried to answer the live-issue question from the Space, or curated-conventions from MCP, you've found the boundary — that's the point.
checklist: MCP server configured and returning live repo data
Space created with repo + docs + notes
A grounded answer that reflects your conventions
You can state which need MCP vs a Space

resources.title: GitHub Copilot features & feature matrix
resources.annotation: Completions, chat, CLI, agents, code review, PR summaries — and where each is available by IDE and plan. The backbone of this domain.

resources.title: Copilot agents — Agent mode, coding agent, Plan mode
resources.annotation: How the coding agent works, plans before acting, and opens a PR you review. Core January-2026 content. Edit mode is on the exam blueprint too (in the product, GitHub is consolidating it into Agent mode).

resources.title: GitHub Copilot CLI
resources.annotation: The terminal-native agent — interactive and plan modes, running shell/git, delegating tasks.

resources.title: Model Context Protocol in Copilot
resources.annotation: The open standard for connecting Copilot to external tools/data; setup in the IDE and CLI.

resources.title: GitHub Copilot Spaces
resources.annotation: Curate and share a bundle of context (repos, files, docs, notes) to ground chat for a task or team.

resources.title: GitHub Spark
resources.annotation: Generate and deploy full-stack apps from natural language. Best for prototypes and internal tools.

resources.title: Copilot code review & PR summaries
resources.annotation: Request an AI review on a PR and draft PR descriptions — assistive tools with humans accountable for the merge.
